1: bᵉnê lēwî — the chapter opens with the primary threefold division of Levi's sons: Gershom (also written Gershon elsewhere; H1647/H1648), Kohath (H6955), and Merari (H4847). The Chronicler consistently uses Gershom rather than Gershon; LXX harmonizes to Gerson throughout. The triad is the structural organizing principle for the entire chapter: the Aaronic high-priestly line runs through Kohath (vv1-15), the broader Levitical families and temple musicians are organized under all three (vv16-48), and the Levitical city allotments in the final section (vv54-81) are distributed among the same three clans.
2: The Kohathite sub-clans — Amram, Izhar, Hebron, Uzziel — are listed in the same order as Exodus 6:18. The priestly family runs through Amram (H6019 — exalted people); Izhar (H3324 — fresh olive oil, he shines), Hebron (H2275 — alliance, league), and Uzziel (H5816 — El is my strength) are the lateral branches. The distinction between the Amramite priestly line and the other Kohathite sub-clans is the structural basis for the Levitical transport legislation in Numbers 3:27-32.
3: yaldê ʿAmrām — 'the children of Amram' uses yālaḏ-derivative yᵉlādîm (H3206, children) rather than the usual bᵉnê (sons), because the list includes Miriam who is female. The same form appears in Numbers 26:59. The inclusion of Miriam alongside Aaron and Moses reflects her prophetic role (Exodus 15:20-21; Micah 6:4). The four sons of Aaron — Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, Ithamar — are listed in birth order; Nadab and Abihu died without sons (Leviticus 10:1-2; Numbers 3:4), so the priestly line continues through Eleazar.
4: wayyôleḏ ʾelʿāzār ʾet pînᵉḥās — the standard genealogical formula switches here to wayyôleḏ (Qal imperfect with waw-consecutive of yālaḏ, H3205 — 'and he fathered'). Phinehas (H6372 — possibly 'serpent's mouth' or an Egyptian loanword pa-nehes meaning 'the Nubian') is the priest who turned back the Baal-Peor plague (Numbers 25:7-13) and received the covenant of eternal priesthood. The Chronicler traces his line to establish the post-exilic Zadokite priesthood's legitimate pedigree.
5: Abishua (H500 — father of salvation) → Bukki (H1231 — proven/tested, or a short form of a longer name) → Uzzi (H5813 — my strength). The chain continues in the same wayyôleḏ formula. These names appear only here and in Ezra 7:4-5 and lack independent historical attestation, but their placement in the chain is essential for the unbroken Aaronic succession.
6: Zerahiah (H2228 — YHWH has risen/shone; from zāraḥ H2224 to rise, of the sun) → Meraioth (H4812 — rebellions or bitter things; from mārah H4784 to be contentious). The name Meraioth's negative semantic load is unusual for a priestly name; names with charged meanings appear in genealogies without implied moral commentary on their bearers.
7: Meraioth → Amariah (H568 — YHWH has said/promised; from ʾāmar + Yāh) → Ahitub (H285 — my brother is good; from ʾāḥ + ṭôḇ). The repeated name Amariah in vv7 and 11, and the repeated Ahitub in vv7 and 11, reflect either genuine name-repetition within the priestly family (common in sacerdotal families honoring ancestors) or a telescoping of the genealogy that has collapsed two distinct figures.
8: Ahitub → Zadok (H6659 — righteous, just; from ṣādaq H6663) — the founding figure of the Jerusalem priestly establishment. Zadok appears first in the narrative as one of David's two high priests (2 Samuel 8:17), then as Solomon's sole high priest after Abiathar's removal (1 Kings 2:27, 35). The Zadokite line becomes the authorized Jerusalem priesthood; Ezekiel 44:15 restricts the restored temple's service to the 'sons of Zadok.' The name ṣādôq gave rise to the term 'Sadducee' (Greek Saddoukaios, Hebrew ṣᵉdôqîm), the Second Temple priestly party claiming descent from this line. Ahimaaz (H290 — my brother is Ammaz/powerful) was the swift messenger who ran to David with news of Absalom's death (2 Samuel 18:19-32).
9: Azariah (H5838 — YHWH has helped; from ʿāzar + Yāh) → Johanan (H3110 — YHWH is gracious; from ḥānan + Yᵉhô). The name Azariah recurs three times in the high-priestly chain (vv9, 10, 13), reflecting its frequency in the priestly line; it is also the birth-name of the king called Uzziah. The alternating theophoric elements (ʿAzaryāh / Yôḥānān / ʿAzaryāh again) create a rhythmic pattern in the name-chain.
10: hûʾ hakkōhēn ʾăšer kihēn bayyêkāl ʾăšer bānāh Šᵉlōmōh bîrûšālāim — 'he is the one who served as priest in the temple that Solomon built in Jerusalem.' The embedded relative clause is a genealogical anchor, identifying one of the Azariahs in the chain as the serving priest in Solomon's temple (probably the Azariah of 1 Kings 4:2). The verb kihēn (H3547, Piel perfect of kāhan — to serve as priest) is the denominal verb from kōhēn; the Piel expresses the functional exercise of the priestly office.
11: A second Azariah → a second Amariah → a second Ahitub. The recurrence of the same names within three generations is a well-attested pattern in priestly families; naming sons after grandfathers maintained the family's genealogical identity and liturgical prestige within the Levitical system. The repetition may also reflect genuine compression of the genealogy by the Chronicler.
12: The second Ahitub → a second Zadok → Shallum (H7967 — recompense; from šālam). This second Zadok is the ancestor of Hilkiah the high priest who found the Torah scroll in Josiah's temple reform (2 Kings 22:8). The genealogy traces the unbroken Zadokite line across the entire monarchic period, establishing continuity of authorized priesthood from David to the exile (v15).
13: Shallum → Hilkiah (H2518 — my portion is YHWH; from ḥēleq + Yāh). This is the Hilkiah who discovered the Torah scroll during Josiah's reform (2 Kings 22:8, 1 Chronicles 23:4). The name's meaning resonates with the Levitical tradition: YHWH himself is the Levites' portion and inheritance (Numbers 18:20; Deuteronomy 18:2), so naming a priest 'my portion is YHWH' is theologically apt. Hilkiah → a third Azariah.
14: The third Azariah → Seraiah (H8304 — YHWH is ruler/prince; from śārar + Yāh). Seraiah is the chief priest at the fall of Jerusalem (2 Kings 25:18), executed by Nebuchadnezzar at Riblah (2 Kings 25:18-21). He fathered Jehozadak (H3087 — YHWH is righteous; Yᵉhô + ṣādaq — a compound of the divine name and the Zadok-root ṣdq). The compound name may reflect the family's identification with its Zadokite priestly heritage.
15: wîhôṣādāq hālaḵ bᵉhaglaôt YHWH ʾet Yᵉhûḏāh wîrûšālāim bᵉyaḏ Nᵉḇûḵaḏneʾṣṣar — 'Jehozadak went when YHWH exiled Judah and Jerusalem by the hand of Nebuchadnezzar.' The verb hālaḵ (H1980 — went) with bᵉhaglaôt (the infinitive construct of gālāh H1540 — to go into exile) uses the standard exile-vocabulary. The phrase bᵉyaḏ Nᵉḇûḵaḏneʾṣṣar (by the hand of Nebuchadnezzar) uses yāḏ (hand) as the instrument of YHWH's action: the Babylonian king is YHWH's yāḏ. The genealogical chain reaches its endpoint; Jehozadak's son Jeshua/Joshua was the high priest who returned with Zerubbabel (Ezra 3:2; Haggai 1:1; Zechariah 3:1).
16: The repetition of 'the sons of Levi were Gershom, Kohath, and Merari' (verbatim repetition of v1) marks the structural seam between the high-priestly genealogy (vv1-15) and the broader Levitical family lists (vv16-30). Structural repetition as a literary device — re-opening a new section with the same formula used to open the chapter — is a recognized technique in biblical genealogical literature, functioning like a section-divider in a legal document.
17: wᵉʾēlleh šᵉmôt bᵉnê gēršôm — 'these are the names of the sons of Gershom.' The name-list (šᵉmôt) formula is distinct from the begetting formula; it introduces a sibling list rather than a generation-chain. Libni (H3845 — white, pale) and Shimei (H8096 — he has heard; from šāmaʿ) are Gershom's two sons, consistent with Exodus 6:17 and Numbers 3:18. Šimʿî is a common name rooted in the concept of hearing/obeying; at least four different people named Shimei appear in Chronicles.
18: The sons of Kohath are repeated from v2 (Amram, Izhar, Hebron, Uzziel) now placed structurally within the three-tribe framework of vv16-30. The repetition is not redundancy but organizational structure: v2 gave the Kohathite clans as the source of the priestly line; here they are situated alongside the Gershomite and Merarite clans as three parallel sections of the full Levitical family list.
19: The sons of Merari — Mahli (H4249 — sick, diseased; or possibly a gentle/mild one) and Mushi (H4187 — drawn from water, or touch; both appear in Exodus 6:19 and Numbers 3:20). The closing formula ʾēlleh mišpᵉḥôt haLēwî lᵉḇêt ʾăḇōtāyw (these are the Levitical families according to their fathers) uses mišpāḥôt (H4940 — families, clans, extended kinship groups) as the organizational unit, distinct from maṭṭeh/šēḇeṭ (tribe) and bêt ʾāḇ (father's house).
20: The Gershomite descent chain uses bᵉnô (his son) as a connector rather than wayyôleḏ, indicating a descent list rather than a strict father-son narrative chain: Libni → Jahath (H3189 — grasping; from ʾāḥaz) → Zimmah (H2155 — lewdness/plot; an unusual name for a Levite, possibly a family name whose negative semantic load had faded in use). The bᵉnô pattern 'X his son, Y his son' (bᵉnô X bᵉnô Y) is a shorthand for representing ancestral succession without the narrative weight of the wayyôleḏ formula.
21: The Gershomite chain continues: Joah (H3098 — YHWH is brother; from Yᵉhô + ʾāḥ) → Iddo (H5714 — timely, at the appointed time; from ʿēd) → Zerah (H2226 — rising/shining; from zāraḥ, the same root as Zerahiah in v6) → Jeaterai (H2979 — who causes to remain, or YHWH causes to remain). The Gershomite line ends at seven names (vv20-21); the same line appears in slightly different form in other lists, reflecting the natural variation in genealogical transmission.
22: The Kohathite line begins here with Amminadab (H5992 — my kinsman is generous/noble; from ʿam + nāḏaḇ) rather than the expected Amram of v2. This is textually significant: Amminadab may be a variant or replacement name for Amram, or a separate figure inserted into the genealogy. His son Korah (H7141 — bald; from qāraḥ) is the leader of the rebellion in Num 16. The Chronicler's genealogical inclusion of Korah without comment reflects the convention of listing all descendants regardless of historical reputation; his descendants become legitimately integrated into temple worship (vv33-38).
23: Korah → Assir (H617 — imprisoned; from ʾāsar) → Elkanah (H511 — God has acquired/created; from ʾēl + qānāh) → Ebiasaph (H43 — my father has gathered; from ʾāḇ + ʾāsap) → Assir (second occurrence). The name Elkanah appears eight times in this chapter, reflecting its frequency in Kohathite families. The double Assir and the chain connecting through Korah to the temple musicians integrates the Korahite guilds (Pss 42-49, 84-88) into the legitimate Levitical structure.
24: Assir → Tahath (H8480 — beneath, underneath; appears twice in this genealogy, vv24 and 37) → Uriel (H222 — God is my flame/light; from ʾûr + ʾēl) → Uzziah (H5818 — YHWH is my strength; same name as the Judean king; from ʿōz + Yāh) → Shaul (H7586 — asked for/prayed for; same name as Israel's first king). The name pattern alternates between descriptive names (Tahath = below) and theophoric names (Uriel, Uzziah).
25: The sons of Elkanah were Amasai (H6022 — my burden; from māśāʾ) and Ahimoth (H287 — my brother is death; from ʾāḥ + māwet). Ahimoth's name is stark: the 'brother of death' formula is unusual in priestly genealogies. Some scholars suggest it may reflect memorial naming — a child born after a sibling's death — a practice attested across ancient cultures. The MT is supported by LXX's Achimoth.
26: From Elkanah: son Zophai (H6689 — honeycomb; from ṣôp; or watchman, from ṣāpāh) → Nahath (H5184 — rest, quiet; or descent). The Elkanah here is a different generation from vv23-25, tracing a lateral branch of the extended Kohathite clan. The repeated name creates apparent confusion but traces multiple collateral lines through the same patriarch.
27: Eliab (H446 — my God is father; from ʾēl + ʾāḇ) → Jeroham (H3395 — he will be compassionate; from riḥam) → Elkanah (the third Elkanah in this section). This Elkanah is Samuel's father (1 Samuel 1:1-2). The descent chain from Kohath → Amram → Elkanah establishes Samuel's Levitical credentials. In 1 Samuel 1:1, Samuel's family is described as from 'the hill country of Ephraim,' indicating their tribal residence, not tribal origin; the Chronicler's genealogy is the authoritative statement of their Levitical descent.
28: ûḇᵉnê šᵉmûʾēl habᵉḵôr waššnî wᵉʾăḇiyyāh — 'the sons of Samuel: the firstborn Vashni (reading MT) and Abijah.' The name Vashni (H2059) is textually problematic: 1 Samuel 8:2 names Samuel's firstborn as Joel (Yôʾēl — YHWH is God) and his second as Abijah. Scholarly consensus is that the MT of Chronicles has suffered a scribal error: the original text read habᵉḵôr Yôʾēl wᵉhašŠēnî (the firstborn Joel and the second), with wahaššēnî (the second) being misread as a name 'Vashni.' Most modern translations (ESV, NIV, NRSV) emend to Joel; the LXX has Joel, supporting the emendation. The MKT preserves the MT reading to flag the textual divergence.
29: The Merarite line: Mahli → son Libni (H3845 — white; the same name as Gershom's son in v17, reflecting cross-clan name repetition) → Shimei → Uzza (H5798 — strength; the same name as the man who touched the ark in 1 Chronicles 13:9-10). The chain uses bᵉnô connectors throughout: 'Mahli, his son Libni, his son Shimei, his son Uzza.'
30: Shimea (H8092 — he has heard; from šāmaʿ; same root as Shimei) → Haggiah (H2293 — festival of YHWH; from ḥag + Yāh; naming a person after a festival day is unusual among theophoric patterns) → Asaiah (H6222 — YHWH has made/done; from ʿāśāh + Yāh). This closes the Merarite genealogy of vv29-30 and completes the three-part family list of vv16-30.
31: wᵉʾēlleh ʾăšer heʿĕmîḏ Dāwîḏ ʿal yᵉḏê šîr bêt YHWH — 'these are the ones whom David stationed over the service of song in the house of the LORD.' The verb heʿĕmîḏ (H5975 Hiphil — caused to stand, stationed, appointed) indicates official installation; David as the organizer of temple worship is a defining theme of Chronicles. The phrase ʿal yᵉḏê šîr (over the hands of song) uses yāḏ idiomatically for 'in charge of/responsible for.' Šîr (H7892 — song, singing) names the musical service; the cognate šîrāh appears in Miriam's song (Exodus 15:1).
32: The musicians 'served with song before the tabernacle of the tent of meeting' — šārat (H8334, Piel — to serve, to minister) is the standard Levitical service verb (distinct from priestly kāhan). The temporal qualifier — 'until Solomon built the house of the LORD in Jerusalem' — frames the tent-of-meeting music as a provisional arrangement superseded by the temple. Kᵉmišpāṭām (according to their prescribed order) uses mišpāṭ (H4941 — judgment, custom, ordinance) for the established liturgical regulation.
33: hēmān hamᵉšōrēr — 'Heman the singer/cantor.' The title mᵉšōrēr (H7891 Poel participle of šîr — one who sings, a cantor) is Heman's defining designation. He is identified as son of Joel son of Samuel — establishing Samuel's grandson as a chief musician, connecting the prophetic (Samuel) and musical (Heman) traditions in Kohathite piety. The Korahite Psalms (Pss 42-49, 84-85, 87-88) are the guild's literary output; Ps 88 is attributed to 'Heman the Ezrahite.'
34: The genealogy traces Heman back through Samuel's line: son of Elkanah → son of Jeroham → son of Eliel (H447 — my God is God; from ʾēl + ʾēl; a double-theophoric name) → son of Toah (H8430 — depression, humility; appears only here). The chain uses ben (son of) ascending from son to father, the reverse of the descending wayyôleḏ pattern.
35: Son of Zuph (H6689 — honeycomb; the same ṣôp root as Zophai in v26; 1 Samuel 1:1 describes Elkanah as 'a Zuphite') → son of Elkanah → son of Mahath (H4287 — smiting; appears only in this genealogy) → son of Amasai (H6022 — cf. v25). The chain works its way back toward Kohath through a lateral line distinct from the direct Amram path.
36: Son of Elkanah (fourth Elkanah) → son of Joel (H3100 — YHWH is God; the same name as Samuel's firstborn in 1 Samuel 8:2, absent from v28 in MT) → son of Azariah → son of Zephaniah (H6846 — YHWH has hidden/protected; from ṣāpan H6845 to hide/treasure + Yāh). The name Zephaniah uses the Qal perfect of ṣāpan (to hide, to store up); the theophoric meaning is 'YHWH has hidden/protected him,' carrying nuances of divine sheltering care.
37: Son of Tahath (H8480 — second occurrence; cf. v24) → son of Assir → son of Ebiasaph (H43 — cf. v23) → son of Korah (H7141 — cf. v22). The chain arrives at Korah, establishing Heman's Korahite identity. The Korahite sons who composed the Korahite psalms are not the rebel Korah himself; Numbers 26:11 records that 'the sons of Korah did not die' when the earth swallowed Korah, and their survival and eventual incorporation into temple worship is precisely what this genealogy charts.
38: Son of Izhar → son of Kohath → son of Levi → son of Israel. The ascending chain arrives at the patriarchal ancestors: Izhar (one of Kohath's four sons, v2), Kohath (v1), Levi, Israel/Jacob. The descent-to-patriarch formula serves as the genealogical anchor. Ben Yiśrāʾēl (son of Israel) closes the ascending chain at the point where all twelve-tribe genealogies terminate, placing Heman's line within the fullest national scope.
39: ûʾăḥîhû ʾāsāp — 'and his colleague Asaph.' The term ʾāḥ (H251 — brother) here means professional colleague rather than biological brother (Heman and Asaph are from different clans: Kohathite vs. Gershomite). Asaph (H623 — he has gathered; from ʾāsap) stood at Heman's right hand — the position of honor. The Asaphite musical guild produced twelve Psalms (Ps 50, 73-83); 1 Chronicles 25:1-2 describes Asaph as 'prophesying' with musical instruments.
40: Asaph's ascending genealogy: son of Berechiah (H1296 — YHWH blesses; from bāraḵ + Yāh) → son of Shimea (H8092 — he has heard; same name as the Merarite in v30) → son of Michael (H4317 — who is like God?; from the rhetorical question mî ḵəʾēl; one of the earliest attestations of this theophoric formula, later the archangel's name in Daniel 10:13) → son of Baaseiah (H1202 — in the work of YHWH; appears only here).
41: Son of Malchijah (H4441 — my king is YHWH; from malkî + Yāh) → son of Ethni (H869 — my gift; appears only here) → son of Zerah (H2226 — rising; cf. vv6 and 21) → son of Adaiah (H5718 — YHWH has adorned/refreshed; from ʿāḏāh H5710 to put on ornaments + Yāh). The name Adaiah uses the relatively rare root ʿāḏāh (to adorn oneself, to put on jewelry), giving the theophoric meaning 'YHWH has adorned himself' or 'YHWH has adorned him.'
42: Son of Ethan (H387 — enduring, long-lived; the same name as the wise man of 1 Kings 4:31 and possibly 'Ethan the Ezrahite' of Ps 89) → son of Zimmah (H2155 — cf. v20) → son of Shimei (H8096 — second occurrence; cf. v17). The multiple Shimeais and Ethansin the genealogy reflect the narrow name-pool in priestly families, where theophoric and virtue-names were recycled across generations.
43: Son of Jahath (H3189 — cf. v20) → son of Gershom → son of Levi. Asaph's chain terminates at Gershom/Levi, confirming his Gershomite clan identity and distinguishing him from Kohathite Heman. The three parallel genealogies (Heman vv33-38, Asaph vv39-43, Ethan vv44-47) represent all three Levitical clans in balanced liturgical arrangement.
44: The third musician: ʾētān (Ethan, H387 — enduring/long-lasting) from the Merarite clan, standing at the left hand. The spatial arrangement — Heman at center, Asaph at the right, Ethan at the left — mirrors liturgical processional formation. Son of Kishi (H6984 — laying YHWH's snare, or bow; from qaš + theophoric element) → son of Abdi (H5660 — my servant; from ʿeḇeḏ) → son of Malluch (H4409 — he has reigned; from mālaḵ).
45: Son of Hashabiah (H2811 — YHWH has thought/considered; from ḥāšaḇ + Yāh — YHWH has reckoned, planned, or valued) → son of Amaziah (H558 — YHWH is strong; from ʾāmaz + Yāh; the same name as several Judean kings) → son of Hilkiah (H2518 — cf. v13). The theophoric naming density in these Levitical genealogies is notably high compared to secular Israelite names, reflecting the priestly-musical guilds' strong YHWH-centered naming tradition.
46: Son of Amzi (H557 — my strength; short form of Amaziah) → son of Bani (H1137 — built; from bānāh — one built/established by God) → son of Shamer (H8106 — guardian, watchman; from šāmar H8104 to keep, guard). The name šāmer (keeper/watchman) uses the same root as the Psalms' descriptions of YHWH as the guardian of Israel who 'neither slumbers nor sleeps' (Psalms 121:4).
47: Son of Mahli (H4249 — cf. v19) → son of Mushi (H4187 — cf. v19) → son of Merari → son of Levi. Ethan's chain terminates at Merari, confirming his Merarite clan identity. The three-part genealogical structure (Heman/Kohath, Asaph/Gershom, Ethan/Merari) is now complete, representing the entire threefold Levitical family in the temple's musical ministry.
48: wᵉʾăḥêhem haLᵉwiyyim — 'their fellow Levites' — were assigned to all other tabernacle/temple service. The term ʾāḥ (brother/colleague) here means professional associates. Kol ʿăḇōdat hāmišᵉkān bêt hāʾelōhîm (all the service of the tabernacle, the house of God) combines two terms: mišᵉkān (H4908 — tabernacle, the dwelling-place; from šākan to dwell) and bêt ʾelōhîm (house of God), treating them as a unified referent spanning from the wilderness sanctuary to Solomon's temple.
49: wᵉʾahᵃrōn ûḇānāyw maqṭîrîm ʿal mizbah hāʿōlāh ûmizbah haqqᵉṭōret — 'But Aaron and his sons were responsible for burning offerings on the altar of burnt offering and the altar of incense.' The adversative wᵉ (but/however) distinguishes Aaronic priestly duties from the Levitical ones of v48. The Hiphil participle maqṭîrîm (causing to smoke; from qāṭar H6999 — to cause sacrificial smoke to rise) is the technical term for altar-burning, distinct from the general verb for burning (śārap). For atonement: lᵉḵappēr ʿal Yiśrāʾēl uses the Piel infinitive of kāpar (H3722 — to cover, to wipe, to make atonement), the root underlying kappōret (the mercy seat/atonement cover, H3727) and Yôm hakkippûrîm (Day of Atonement). The precise liturgical citation — 'exactly as Moses the servant of God had commanded' (kᵉḵōl ʾăšer ṣiwwāh Mōšeh ʿeḇeḏ hāʾelōhîm) — is characteristically Chronistic, anchoring temple practice in Mosaic authority.
50: The second Aaronic genealogy (vv50-53) parallels vv4-8 in a compressed name-list format rather than the narrative wayyôleḏ chain. ʾēlleh bᵉnê ʾahᵃrōn introduces the list: Eleazar → Phinehas → Abishua (H500 — cf. v4). The double attestation of the Eleazar-to-Ahimaaz chain — once in narrative-begetting form (vv4-9) and once in name-list form (vv50-53) — provides a second witness to the priestly succession that the Chronicler considered essential for the post-exilic community's authorization of legitimate priesthood.
51: Abishua → Bukki (H1231 — cf. v5) → Uzzi (H5813 — cf. v5) → Zerahiah (H2228 — cf. v6). Four generations compressed into a single verse. The same names appear in Ezra 7:4 in the priestly genealogy Ezra provides for his own ancestry, confirming the Chronicler's genealogical data was used in post-exilic legal contexts.
52: Zerahiah → Meraioth (H4812 — cf. v6) → Amariah (H568 — cf. v7) → Ahitub (H285 — cf. v7). The name Meraioth (mᵉrāyôt, grammatical plural of mᵉrî — rebelliousness) appears only in Chronicles and Nehemiah, exclusively in priestly genealogical lists. Its plural form may be honorific (an abstract plural of excellence) rather than indicating actual plural referents.
53: Ahitub → Zadok (H6659 — cf. v8) → Ahimaaz (H290 — cf. v8). The second Aaronic genealogy ends at Ahimaaz, the same endpoint as the chain-anchor in v9. This creates a deliberate double-witness structure for the most important segment of the priestly genealogy: Zadok → Ahimaaz is attested twice, once in narrative form (v8-9) and once in list form (v53), reinforcing the legitimacy of the founding Zadokite lineage.
54: wᵉʾēlleh mᵉšiḇôtām lᵉṭîrôtām bigḇûlām — 'these are their dwelling places according to their allotted territories.' The term mᵉšiḇôtām (H4186 — dwelling places, settlements; from yāšaḇ to dwell) is paired with ṭîrôtām (territories/enclosures) within their gᵉḇûlām (boundaries). The lot system (gôrāl H1486 — lot, casting of lots) was the divinely-sanctioned allocation mechanism for both tribal land and Levitical city assignment; its use signals that the city allotments are YHWH's determination, not human negotiation.
55: The first Aaronic allocation: Hebron (Ḥeḇrôn H2275 — alliance, league) in Judah's territory with its migrāšêhā (pasturelands). Migrāš (H4054 — common land, pastureland; from gāraš H1644 to drive away, used for land cleared/reserved for grazing) is the technical term for the open land surrounding Levitical cities, specified in Numbers 35:4-5 as extending 1,000 cubits from the city wall. This term appears approximately 20 times in vv54-81, becoming the chapter's dominant vocabulary for the city-allotment section.
56: The distinction between Aaronic city-territory and Caleb's fields: ûśᵉḏê hāʿîr wᵉḥaṣᵉrêhā nātᵉnû lᵉḵāleḇ ben Yᵉpunneh — 'the open fields and villages of Hebron were given to Caleb son of Jephunneh.' Śāḏeh (H7704 — field, open country) and ḥāṣēr (H2691 — enclosure, courtyard, unwalled village) designate the productive agricultural lands outside the city proper. The dual allocation — city to priests, fields to Caleb — reflects Joshua 14:13-14's fulfillment of the Mosaic promise.
57: The cities of refuge (ʿārê hammiqlaṭ; miqlaṭ H4733 — place of shelter/asylum; from qālaṭ to shelter) assigned to the Aaronites. Hebron (Joshua 20:7), Libnah (H3841 — white; in the Shephelah), Jattir (H3492 — pre-eminent; Negev highlands, 1 Samuel 30:27), and Eshtemoa (H851 — I will make myself heard). The six cities of refuge (three east of Jordan, three west, Joshua 20:7-8) are a subset of the 48 Levitical cities; the overlap between refuge cities and Aaronic cities is specified in Numbers 35:6.
58: Hilen (H2432 — strength; called Holon in Joshua 21:15) and Debir (H1688 — oracle/word; a prominent Judean city captured by Othniel, Joshua 15:15-17). The ûmigrāšêhā formula (and its pasturelands) appended to each city name appears in every subsequent verse of this section, creating the standardized legal language derived from Num 35 and Josh 21.
59: Ashan (H6228 — smoke; Joshua 15:42) and Beth-shemesh (H1053 — house of the sun; a city associated with the ark's return from Philistia, 1 Samuel 6:9-20). Beth-shemesh's inclusion in the Aaronic city list is theologically resonant: the ark returned through this city, and the men of Beth-shemesh died for looking into the ark (1 Samuel 6:19). A priestly city and an ark-transit city are here co-identified, placing the Aaronic custodial responsibility over this ark-associated site.
60: Three cities from Benjamin: Geba (H1387 — hill; north of Jerusalem), Alemeth (H5964 — covering, concealment; Joshua 21:18 calls it Almon), and Anathoth (H6068 — answers; the priestly city famous as Jeremiah's birthplace, Jeremiah 1:1). Anathoth's priestly character explains the village's identity: Jeremiah is a priest by birth (Jeremiah 1:1 identifies him as 'of the priests who were in Anathoth'). Total for the Aaronic Kohathite allotment: thirteen cities, consistent with Joshua 21:4.
61: The remaining (non-Aaronic) Kohathites received ten cities from the half-tribe of Manasseh west of the Jordan. Hannôtārîm (H3498 Niphal participle of yātar — what remains, the remainder) distinguishes these Kohathites from the Aaronic priestly family. The miḥᵃṣî maṭṭēh manašṣeh (half-tribe of Manasseh) designation reflects the bifurcation of Manasseh between Transjordanian (east) and Cisjordanian (west) settlements.
62: The Gershomite allotment — thirteen cities from Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and eastern Manasseh (Bashan) — spans the northern tribal territories. Baššān (H1316 — fertile plateau; the region east of the Sea of Galilee famous for its oak forests and cattle, Psalms 22:12; Amos 4:1) was the Gershomites' Transjordanian allotment. The number thirteen matches Joshua 21:6.
63: The Merarite allotment — twelve cities by lot from Reuben, Gad, and Zebulun. Wayyᵉṣᵉʾû bᵉgôrāl (they came out by lot) uses the standard lot-formula. The Merarite cities span the Transjordanian territories (Reuben, Gad) plus the Galilee coastal territory (Zebulun), a geographically dispersed assignment consistent with the Levites' distributed presence across all Israel — precisely the design of Num 35's Levitical city legislation.
64: wayyittᵉnû bᵉnê Yiśrāʾēl laLᵉwiyyim ʾet hāʿārîm wᵉʾet migrāšêhem — 'the Israelites gave the Levites the cities with their pasturelands.' The subject is the entire Israelite community acting in corporate faithfulness to Mosaic legislation (Numbers 35:2-3). The migrāšêhem formula appears in this summary statement, reinforcing that the pastureland is an integral and non-negotiable component of the Levitical city grant.
65: Wayyittᵉnû bᵉgôrāl (they assigned by lot) — the lot mechanism again; the cities from Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin are re-enumerated as given by lot from these three southern tribes. The repetition in vv64-65 — a general allotment summary followed by a specific southern-tribe clarification — reflects the two-step structure of Josh 21's account.
66: Some Kohathite families also received cities from Ephraim's territory. Ûmimišpᵉḥôt bᵉnê qᵉhāt (and from the families of the sons of Kohath) picks up the non-Aaronic Kohathites whose Manassite cities were mentioned in v61. The Ephraim-territory Kohathite cities placed legitimate priestly-adjacent families throughout the central highland region.
67: The cities of refuge in Ephraim: Shechem (H7927 — shoulder/back; the central hill-country city where the Mosaic covenant was renewed in Josh 24; Joshua 20:7 designates it as a city of refuge) and Gezer (H1507 — portion cut off; a strategic Shephelah city). Shechem's dual designation — covenant-renewal site and city of refuge — makes it the most theologically layered city in this list.
68: Jokmeam (H3362 — let the people rise up; from qûm + ʿam; called Kibzaim in Joshua 21:22) and Beth-horon (H1032 — house of the hollow/cave; actually two linked cities — upper and lower Beth-horon — controlling the main ascent route from the coastal plain toward Jerusalem). Beth-horon's military significance made it a repeated site of decisive engagement in Israelite history (Joshua 10:10-11; 1 Samuel 13:18; 1 Macc 1 Chronicles 3:16-24).
69: Aijalon (H357 — deer-place; from ʾayyāl a stag/deer; the valley where the sun stood still in Joshua 10:12-13, one of the most dramatic divine interventions in the conquest narratives) and Gath-rimmon (H1667 — winepress of the pomegranate; from gat + rimmôn). Both are in the Shephelah; these were originally Danite cities that became Kohathite Levitical cities after Dan's migration north (Judg 18).
70: From the western half-tribe of Manasseh: Aner (H6063 — waterfall; appears only in Levitical city lists) and Bileam (H1109 — lord of the people; called Ibleam in Joshua 21:25, overlooking the Jezreel Valley). The note that these were 'for the remaining Kohathite families' (lîtèr mišpaḥat bᵉnê qᵉhāt) supplements the eastern Manassite allotment of v61.
71: The Gershomite allotment from eastern Manasseh/Bashan: Golan (H1474 — exile, carried away; the elevated plateau east of the Sea of Galilee, a city of refuge Joshua 20:8; the modern Golan Heights preserves the ancient name) and Ashtaroth (H6252 — Astartes, plural; a Canaanite goddess-name used as a city-name in Bashan, modern Tell Ashtara in Syria). The irony of a Levitical city named for the goddess Astarte is not commented on by the Chronicler — the name was purely geographic by this period.
72: From Issachar: Kedesh (H6943 — sanctuary, holy; distinct from Kedesh in Naphtali, v76) and Daberath (H1705 — word, pasture; at the base of Mt. Tabor, Joshua 21:28). The ûmigrāšêhā formula continues with mechanical regularity through vv72-81, reflecting the legal formula's standardized character throughout the Levitical city legislation.
73: Ramoth (H7216 — heights; in Issachar, distinct from Ramoth-gilead) and Anem (H6046 — two fountains, dual of ʿayin spring/eye; Joshua 21:29 calls it En-gannim). The two-city-per-verse format reflects both the oral-transmission pattern of legal lists and the Chronicler's condensed treatment relative to the fuller Josh 21 account.
74: From Asher: Mashal (H4913 — proverb/saying; called Mishal in Joshua 21:30) and Abdon (H5658 — service, servitude; from ʿāḇad). Asher's territory ran along the Mediterranean coast north of Carmel; the Asherite Levitical cities secured priestly presence along the coastal corridor that was the zone of Phoenician commercial influence.
75: Hukok (H2712 — engraved or portion; called Helkath in Joshua 21:31) and Rehob (H7340 — broad place, plaza; from rāḥaḇ). The name Rehob (wide open space) was applied to multiple towns; the Asherite Rehob is in the Acco plain. The toponym rᵉḥōḇ (broad/open space) is the same root as the placename Rehoboth and describes a naturally wide area, likely a valley junction or agricultural flatland.
76: From Naphtali: Kedesh in Galilee (Qāḏēš baggālîl — Kedesh in the Galil; the full designation distinguishing this northern city of refuge from the Issacharite Kedesh of v72; Joshua 20:7), Hammon (H2540 — warm springs; near the Mediterranean coast north of Acco), and Kiriathaim (H7156 — double city; dual form of qiryāh city; called Kartan in Joshua 21:32). 'Galilee' (gālîl H1551 — circle, district) here refers to the northern highland region; it is the territory whose first Assyrian deportation (2 Kings 15:29) is later addressed by Isaiah 9:1.
77: The Merarite allotment in Zebulun: Rimmono (H7417 — pomegranate; called Rimmon in Joshua 21:35) and Tabor (H8396 — mountain/height; Mt. Tabor, the distinctive isolated volcanic hill c. 600m high in the Jezreel Valley — a natural landmark and traditional worship site). The designation habbnôtārîm laLᵉwiyyim ûlibᵉnê mᵉrārî (the remaining Levites, the sons of Merari) mirrors the 'remaining Kohathites' formula of v61.
78: From Reuben, east of the Jordan opposite Jericho: Bezer (H1221 — inaccessible fortress; a city of refuge in the wilderness plateau, Deuteronomy 4:43; Joshua 20:8). The descriptor bammidbar (in the wilderness/desert; H4057 — uninhabited land, steppe) designates the Moabite plateau east of the Dead Sea. Jahzah (H3096 — trodden down; where Sihon of Heshbon was defeated in Numbers 21:23) marks the border territory of the tribal allotment.
79: Kedemoth (H6932 — ancient/eastern places; the area from which Moses sent messengers to Sihon requesting passage, Deuteronomy 2:26) and Mephaath (H4158 — splendor or heights; in the Reubenite tableland). These four Reubenite cities — Bezer, Jahzah, Kedemoth, Mephaath — correspond to Joshua 21:36-37's Reubenite Merarite allotment.
80: From Gad: Ramoth in Gilead (H7433 — heights of Gilead; the major Transjordanian city of refuge east of the Jordan; contested between Israel and Aram throughout 1-2 Kings, from Ahab's death at Ramoth-gilead in 1 Kgs 22 to Jehu's anointing there in 2 Kgs 9) and Mahanaim (H4266 — two camps; where Jacob encountered the angels in Genesis 32:2, and where David retreated during Absalom's revolt, 2 Samuel 17:24).
81: Heshbon (H2809 — stronghold; the Amorite capital under Sihon, Numbers 21:25-26; later in Gadite territory) and Jazer (H3270 — he helps; in the Ammonite borderland, Numbers 21:32). The Levitical city list ends here — 48 cities total across all twelve tribes (Numbers 35:7 specifies 48 as the required total). The chapter's spatial closure at Jazer and Heshbon in the Transjordanian territory mirrors the chapter's opening in the Cisjordanian tribal heartland: Levi's service covers the entire expanse of the promised land from coast to eastern plateau.
